---
title: "Identifying Issues and Formulating Questions"
description: "Study note on Identifying Issues and Formulating Questions in Evaluation: A Systematic Approach."
book_id: "evaluation-systematic-approach"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Major section"
source_pages: "89-90"
citation_key: "rossi_lipsey_freeman_2004_evaluation_systematic_approach"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:evaluation-systematic-approach:ch03-chapter-3-identifying-issues-and-formulating-questions:c0055"
source_status: "needs_review"
---
## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2004</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Source pages 89-90</span><span>Major section</span></div>
This note summarises the MCP research-library section "Identifying Issues and Formulating Questions" from Peter H. Rossi, Mark W. Lipsey, and Howard E. Freeman's *Evaluation: A Systematic Approach* [@rossi_lipsey_freeman_2004_evaluation_systematic_approach]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked retrieval records, not a substitute for the source text.

## Core Argument

The section stresses that evaluation questions organize design, analysis, and eventual use.

## Study Summary

The section stresses that evaluation questions organize design, analysis, and eventual use.

Questions may matter even when immediate instrumental use is not obvious, because evaluation can shape understanding and debate.

The evaluator must therefore prioritize questions for practical decision value and conceptual influence.

## Key Concepts

- Question formulation
- Prioritization
- Conceptual use
- Persuasive use

## Source Location

- Section path: Chapter 3: Identifying Issues and Formulating Questions
- Evidence records: `chunk:evaluation-systematic-approach:ch03-chapter-3-identifying-issues-and-formulating-questions:c0055`

## Practical Implications

- Separate interesting questions from decision-critical questions.
- Keep questions broad enough to support learning but narrow enough to answer.
- Document why lower-priority questions were deferred.

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Concepts: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Map: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What is the main evaluation problem that Identifying Issues and Formulating Questions helps diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before using this guidance in a live evaluation?
3. How would this change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, or reporting?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## References
